title: Two-route evacuation card
summary: A pocket card with a primary route, backup route, meeting points, helpers, and no-phone instructions.
content: Evacuation fails when the only plan is inside one phone or one person's head. The card names the danger, trigger to leave, primary route, backup route, near meeting point, far meeting point, who helps whom, and what to do if phones fail.
steps: Choose two routes that do not share the same choke point.
Write the trigger for leaving early.
Name helpers for children, elderly people, pets, or disabled people.
Add near and far meeting points.
warnings: Do not wait if official evacuation is ordered.
Do not rely on elevators during fire, earthquake damage, or power instability.
Do not return into danger for supplies.
common_mistakes: Two routes that cross the same bridge.
Card kept only in an inaccessible bag.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Two-route evacuation card with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.
Use a safer fallback location or method for Two-route evacuation card if the first choice becomes wet, crowded, contaminated, dark, unstable, or unsafe.
Pause Two-route evacuation card and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.
failure_signs: People cannot explain who is responsible for Two-route evacuation card or when the next check happens.
Supplies connected to Two-route evacuation card are unlabelled, damp, contaminated, damaged, missing, or being used for the wrong purpose.
The routine ignores clear records, verified messages, offline copies, and review times and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Two-route evacuation card”
when_not_to_use: Do not use Two-route evacuation card as a reason to delay evacuation, emergency calls, medical care, fire response, or official local instructions.
Do not use Two-route evacuation card when the location is structurally unsafe, chemically suspicious, smoky, flooded, violent, or otherwise immediately dangerous.
Do not improvise hazardous repairs, dosing, chemical handling, weapon use, or electrical/gas work under the label of Two-route evacuation card.
short_term: For the first hours of Two-route evacuation card, keep the action small, visible, and reversible: write the goal, assign one owner, protect vulnerable people, separate safe from unsafe supplies, and set a review time.
long_term: If Two-route evacuation card remains necessary for days or weeks, rotate roles, preserve written lessons, replace fragile supplies, and train another person so the group does not depend on one expert.
if_method_fails: If Two-route evacuation card fails, stop escalation, move to the safest fallback, record what failed, protect people first, and restart only with a simpler written plan.
environment_notes: Adapt Two-route evacuation card to the real environment: check clear records, verified messages, offline copies, and review times, local weather, building condition, water access, light, noise, animals, neighbours, and safe exits before repeating the routine.
